summary:
The Imjin War (1592–1598 AD) was Japan's invasion of Korea — the largest military conflict in East Asian history up to that time — launched by the Japanese warlord [[event:3237|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] with approximately 158,000 soldiers, nominally aimed at conquering China through Korea but ultimately resulting in a failed invasion that devastated the Korean peninsula while consuming Japanese military resources, and whose reverberations destabilized the political balance of East Asia. Hideyoshi's ambition was continental empire: having unified Japan, he sought to channel the military energy of his recently pacified samurai into foreign conquest. Korea refused to serve as a passage route; Japan invaded anyway, landing at Busan in May 1592 and advancing northward with extraordinary speed — Seoul fell in three weeks; Pyongyang fell weeks later.

The invasion's initial success owed much to Japanese arquebusiers (matchlock muskets), which Korean forces had never encountered. However, the tide turned through three factors: Korean Admiral Yi Sun-sin's ironclad turtle ships (geobukseon) devastated Japanese naval resupply lines in a series of naval victories, particularly at the Battle of Hansando; Chinese Ming forces intervened in force with approximately 50,000 troops; and Korean guerrilla fighters (righteous armies, or uibyeong) harassed Japanese supply lines from behind. A negotiated armistice (1593–1597) collapsed, and Hideyoshi launched a second invasion in 1597 that achieved even less; his death in 1598 allowed the Japanese forces to withdraw. Korea was devastated: an estimated 100,000–200,000 Korean civilians were killed or enslaved; Korean pottery kilns and craftsmen were forcibly relocated to Japan (explaining the origin of several famous Japanese pottery traditions). The war strained Ming China fatally and contributed to [[event:2374|Ming decline]]; it radicalized Japanese samurai culture and contributed to the political instability that led to the [[event:2421|Battle of Sekigahara]].
